steps.title: Identify the angle at P using collinearity
steps.content:
The marked angle at P is $60^{\circ}$. Since M, P, and Q lie on the same line (diagonal $\overline{MQ}$), the angles on either side of this line at P are supplementary.

The marked angle appears to be $\angle QPR = 60^{\circ}$, so the supplementary angle on the other side is $\angle MPR = 180^{\circ} - 60^{\circ} = 120^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Apply isosceles triangle properties to triangle PMR
steps.content: Triangle PMR has $MP = PR$ (given), making it isosceles. With the vertex angle $\angle MPR = 120^{\circ}$, the two base angles must be equal. Since the angles in a triangle sum to $180^{\circ}$: $$\angle PMR + \angle PRM + 120^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}$$ $$\angle PMR + \angle PRM = 60^{\circ}$$ $$\angle PMR = \angle PRM = 30^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Find ∠QMR using point collinearity
steps.content:
Since P lies on segment $\overline{MQ}$ (between M and Q), the ray from M through P points directly toward Q.

Therefore, $\angle QMR$ and $\angle PMR$ are the same angle (the angle at M measured from the line MQ to the line MR).

Thus $\angle QMR = \angle PMR = 30^{\circ}$. That matches a grid answer of 30.
